entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: 5 injured in crash involving CPD squad car
entries.summary: <p>Five people were injured after a CPD squad car was involved in a car crash in West Garfield Park.</p><p>Two Chicago police officers were responding to a call when a driver failed to yield to their sirens at an intersection in West Garfield Park and caused a crash, Chicago police said.</p><p>The officers were driving in a marked squad car with their lights and sirens on just after midnight in the 3800 block of West Washington Boulevard. A 42-year-old man driving a gray SUV failed to yield to the officers and crashed into the squad car, according to police. Three parked cars also were damaged in the crash.</p><p>The driver and two male passengers were taken to Mount Sinai Hospital with minor injuries. Two officers were in good condition at a local hospital.</p><p>The drive was also taken into custody and charges are pending, according to police.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Ex-Cub Kyle Hendricks in no rush to figure out what comes after baseball; says, 'Not knowing, it's awesome'
entries.summary: <p>A year ago on April 13, Kyle Hendricks had his first rough go with his boyhood team, the Angels, taking it on the chin in Houston for his first loss of his only major league season pitching for anyone other than the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="_blank" >Cubs</a>.</p><p>There would be more games like that in the ensuing months, enough that when Hendricks hit the finish line with an 8-10 record and 4.76 ERA at age 35, he decided to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2025/11/10/cubs-great-kyle-hendricks-set-to-retire-from-playing-after-12-major-league-seasons" target="_blank" >shut it down for good</a>, calling it a career with a 105-91 record that probably didn’t do justice to how excellent he’d been at his finest in 2016 and for four or five seasons after that.</p><p>But the whole lot was enough for him. Now 36, he’s enjoying what he calls a “transition stage” in Arizona, where he’s a stay-at-home dad to his and wife Emma’s 2-year-old son, Luca.</p><p>“I would love to grow the family, but right now, really it’s just nice to not have everything be crazy,” he told the Sun-Times. “Your whole life being baseball, it’s always just go, go, go. I feel so lucky, all the experiences I had.”</p><p>Those who covered “the Professor’s” Cubs tenure, which lasted 11 seasons, saw the bookish, whip-smart Ivy Leaguer as a natural to move into coaching or even onto a front-office track after his pitching days, but Hendricks isn’t sure that’s for him.</p><p>“It’s amazing to me that people even thought that, but my dream was to be a player,” he said. “You know the game comes to an end at some point. You’re still so young. At that point, you’re blessed with what you get.”</p><p>But maybe?</p><p>“I’m not writing anything off, for sure,” he said. “But I knew I was going to be a player. Now, not knowing, it is awesome. Of course I love baseball, love the game, loved pitching. But coaching? All those days away? I don’t want to do any of that. But I’m not closing any doors.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2025/02/14/kyle-hendricks-angels-cubs-perry-minasian-mlb-spring-training-tempe" >Confident Kyle Hendricks — a ‘lock’ for Angels’ rotation, GM says — eager to prove himself again</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2023/6/16/23764118/cubs-kyle-hendricks-contract-jed-hoyer-david-ross" >Cubs’ Kyle Hendricks on uncertain future: ‘I want to be part of this for as long as I can’</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2016/11/2/18401949/cubs-hendricks-the-right-guy-at-the-right-time-for-game-7-task" target="_blank" >Cubs’ Hendricks: the right guy at the right time for Game 7 task</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>One supposes Hendricks could always become an astronaut. That’s not him talking, but rather a writer who remembers him in 2016 — when he led the majors with a 2.13 ERA and took the mound for the Cubs in Game 7 of the World Series — reading diligently about outer space in between starts. He also was hopelessly hooked on the Netflix sci-fi show “Stranger Things.” A mish-mash of intellectual curiosity and regular-Joe-ness, he was.</p><p>As the Cubs celebrate their only championship since 1908 throughout this season, it’s worth remembering how vital a pitcher who overhauled his mechanics, adding a killer curveball and four-seam fastball to his previous mix, was to the whole 2016 shebang. Hendricks trailed Jon Lester, John Lackey and Jake Arrieta around like a puppy for much of that season — the dinners, none of which he dared take the lead on, were legendary — but he became one of them and more than held up his end.</p><p>And in Game 7 in Cleveland, when home starter Corey Kluber had a chance to become the first pitcher to win three starts in a World Series since Detroit’s Mickey Lolich in 1968, it was Hendricks who stared down what he’d called, the night before, the “ultimate dream” and delivered.</p><p>Cubs manager Joe Maddon infamously yanked Hendricks from that game in the fifth inning, even though the stoic righty was rolling along. Hendricks never has complained publicly about the missed chance at earning the win in the biggest Cubs game of ’em all, but now he’s kicking back with nothing to lose.</p><p>So how about it?</p><p>“Nah,” he said. “I’ve never changed with that, really never have. It was Game 7, the heat of the moment. It was how it was supposed to go. Who knows? If I would have stayed in the game, maybe a couple pitches here and there and then — boom — give up a homer.”</p><p>A gracious take. And very much in character, as ever.</p><p>Hendricks had an 18-start streak of allowing two or fewer earned runs that season, which boggles the mind. He outdueled Dodgers great Clayton Kershaw in the National League pennant clincher at Wrigley Field. He was automatic — and the most surprisingly great Cubs player of all in the year that changed everything.</p><p>One would hope he has put his feet up and watched his 26-year-old self do all that.</p><p>But no, not entirely.</p><p>“I did allow myself, I will say, to watch a lot of the World Series on MLB Network, the key moments I hadn’t watched,” he said. “I’ve sat around with my family and enjoyed that.</p><p>“But the whole season? No, I haven’t. I think it’s still too close. I haven’t gotten selfish in that way. But I think eventually I will. I’ll want to see that, especially with my son.”</p><p>The son of an astronaut? Probably not.</p><p>But there’s a stay-at-home dad with an other-worldly story to share.</p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: One person killed, another injured in Auburn Gresham shooting
entries.summary: <p>A woman was killed and a man was injured after someone fired into a group of people in Auburn Gresham late Saturday, Chicago police said.</p><p>The shooting happened around 11:45 p.m. Saturday in the 8000 block of South Morgan Street, according to police. A 25-year-old woman was shot several times and taken to University of Chicago Medical Center, where she was later pronounced dead. A 36-year-old man was shot in the abdomen and leg, and taken in fair condition to Advocate Christ Medical Center.</p><p>No one else from the group was injured, and the shooter fled in a silver sedan, authorities said. No arrests have been made, according to police.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: 3 hospitalized after Irving Park shooting
entries.summary: <p>There men were shot in Irving Park early Sunday morning, Chicago police said.</p><p>The men were outside around 2:45 a.m. in the 2900 block of West Irving Park Road when an unknown female opened fire at them from a distance, according to police.</p><p>A 37-year-old man suffered multiple gunshot wounds and was taken in critical condition to Advocate Illinois Masonic Medical Center. The other two victims were in fair condition, according to police. A 31-year-old man was shot in the calf and taken to Saint Francis Hospital in Evanston, and a 38-year-old man was shot in the leg and taken to Illinois Masonic.</p><p>No one has been arrested, police said.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: WNBA free agency update: Courtney Vandersloot resigns with the Sky
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> resigned Courtney Vandersloot, who is expected to return from her ACL injury this season.</p><p>“I truly believe the Sky are building something special, which made it a no-brainer to sign back,” Vandersloot said in a statement. “Finishing last season with an injury was disappointing, but I’m eager to be back on the court.”</p><p>Vandersloot, 37, is arguably the Sky's best player of all time. She leads the Sky in total points, assists, and steals. She's also second in all-time assists in league history, behind only Sue Bird.</p><p>The Sky back court now includes Vandersloot, Skylar Diggins, Jacy Sheldon and Hailey Van Lith. </p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Chicago set for taste of summer as temps hit 80s, bringing a chance of showers through Saturday
entries.summary: <p>This week will bring a taste of summer with temperatures expected to be in the lower 80s in some spots, and along with the high temperatures will come “wave after wave after wave” of rain and severe thunderstorms, according to Brett Borchardt, a senior meteorologist with the National Weather Service.</p><p>Sunday night is expected to see “waves” of thunder and rain, though not as strong as other recent storms that have hit the area, Borchardt said.</p><p>“It’s not gonna be a wash out or raining all night,” he said.</p><p>Temperatures are expected to jump to 78 on Monday and 80 on Tuesday, staying in the mid 70s and low 80s through the end of the week.</p><p>While Monday is likely to remain dry, Tuesday and Wednesday’s conditions will be particularly favorable for severe weather, Borchardt said. He urged people to stay prepared and have multiple ways to receive storm information.</p><p>“It’s gonna be a window to watch; we could have some pretty strong storms or possibly tornadoes,” he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Showers increase in coverage later today through the evening with a few storms possible. Waves of showers and storms then continue through midweek, however, there will still be plenty of dry hours! Temps will be unseasonably warm with highs in the 70s and lower 80s. <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/ILwx?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#ILwx</a> <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/INwx?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#INwx</a> <a href="https://t.co/nPvhBu63bk">pic.twitter.com/nPvhBu63bk</a></p>— NWS Chicago (@NWSChicago) <a href="https://twitter.com/NWSChicago/status/2043285507891716539?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 12, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Sunday’s anticipated quarter inch of rain isn’t likely to cause flooding near the Fox and Kankakee rivers, which were both still elevated from previous rain and prompted flood warnings near them, but the risk could rise as the week progresses.</p><p>There’s a chance for storms to hit the city almost every day through Saturday, meaning the ground will have less capacity to absorb the rainfall.</p><p>“We could start to have issues with flooding, especially by Thursday as the ground gets more saturated,” Borchardt said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">A summer-like pattern sets up across the region this week. In addition to warmer temps and higher humidity there will be multiple chances for showers/storms, some potentially severe (particularly TUE-WED). Some uncertainty in the details remains though. Stay tuned! <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/ILwx?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#ILwx</a> <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/INwx?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#INwx</a> <a href="https://t.co/cHOLcodVMh">pic.twitter.com/cHOLcodVMh</a></p>— NWS Chicago (@NWSChicago) <a href="https://twitter.com/NWSChicago/status/2043285217629048901?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 12, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Over the past century in Chicago, the likelihood of heavy rainstorms — fueled in part by climate change — has increased sevenfold, often overwhelming the city’s sewers. That’s while federal relief money remains unspent, leaving the thousands whose homes flood without help or answers, according to a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/10/flooding-chicago-climate-change-deep-tunnel-mold-metropolitan-water-reclamation-district-soaked" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Sun-Times investigation</u></a>.</p><p>The summer-like pattern will come to an end toward the start of next week, when temperatures are expected to return to highs in the upper 50s and lower 60s through next week, which is more typical for this time of year, Borchardt said.</p><p>He said no snow is forecast, so Chicagoans may have <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/04/10/signs-spring-chicago-what-you-told-us" target="_blank" ><u>finally made it to spring</u></a> — for real this time.</p><p>“But with each cold front, the floor for temperatures rises as you get toward summer,” he added.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/10/flooding-chicago-climate-change-deep-tunnel-mold-metropolitan-water-reclamation-district-soaked" >Flooding in Chicago is getting worse. Here’s why.</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/04/10/signs-spring-chicago-what-you-told-us" >What’s a clear sign of spring in Chicago? Here’s what you told us</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/consumer-affairs/2026/03/30/sba-low-interest-disaster-loans-residents-businesses-last-year-severe-storms" >SBA offering low-interest loans to residents, businesses impacted by last year’s severe storms</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Rain delays start of White Sox-Royals on Sunday
entries.summary: <p>KANSAS CITY, Mo. — A rainy Kansas City forecast has delayed the start of the White Sox game against the Royals on Sunday. </p><p>There wasn't estimated start time for the game, which was originally scheduled for 1:10 p.m. Showers were in the forecast for much of the afternoon. </p><p>First pitch was delayed by 25 minutes on Saturday due to rain as well. </p><p>The Sox are vying for a split in the four-game series, which has seen Chicago bats go silent <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/11/white-sox-royals-shutout-kansas-city" >in two straight shutouts</a>. </p><p>But fans are already looking forward to Tuesday's home game against the Rays, when top pitching prospect and Oswego East product Noah Schultz <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/12/white-sox-call-up-noah-schultz-sam-antonacci" >will make his MLB debut</a>, the team announced. </p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Blackhawks hope individual improvement by every kid adds up to teamwide improvement next season
entries.summary: <p>Right now, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> have hit a wall entering their final two games of the season. Coach Jeff Blashill has acknowledged that several times in recent days.</p><p>Right now, it's difficult to imagine this same Hawks team competing for a playoff spot next season, considering how much they've <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/11/blackhawks-silly-sam-lafferty-defense-blues-ethan-del-mastro-jeff-blashill" target="_blank" >struggled</a> and how little the roster will change during the offseason.</p><p>Right now, fan anxiety seems to be reaching an all-time high, at least in the time since general manager Kyle Davidson launched this rebuild four years ago.</p><p>So what gives Davidson and the organization belief that the medium-term — not just long-term — outlook is still promising? How could this team actually compete for playoffs next season?</p><p>The first ingredient will be a couple major summer additions. Even if the roster doesn't change much overall, the top of it needs to change.</p><p>Russian prospect Roman Kantserov, who just led the KHL in goal-scoring as a 21-year-old, will be one of those additions. His upside is extremely high, and he will likely slide right into a first- or second-line winger spot.</p><p>An established NHL star needs to be another addition. Davidson hinted after the trade deadline that he's finally eager to make a splash, although he tries hard to keep expectations low.</p><p>If the last remaining prominent free agent (Alex Tuch) or any of the three prominent forwards who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/14/blackhawks-nhl-trade-possibilities-robert-thomas-jason-robertson-matthew-knies" target="_blank" >might get traded</a> (Jason Robertson, Robert Thomas and Matthew Knies) end up changing teams, Davidson needs to do everything within his power to make the Hawks the team they move to.</p><p>The second ingredient will be internal improvement by all the young players already on the Hawks' roster.</p><p>All 15 or so of them are poised to take substantial developmental leaps over the summer. They're at the ages where strength, speed and emotional maturity can change dramatically over the course of five months. </p><p>They also finally have NHL experience they can use to inform their training decisions, narrowing their focus to specific areas where they noticed the largest gap between themselves and elite competition. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Ryan Greene with a sneaky little wrister to tie things up! <a href="https://t.co/TDijF9Ch0Y">pic.twitter.com/TDijF9Ch0Y</a></p>— Blackhawks on CHSN (@CHSN_Blackhawks) <a href="https://twitter.com/CHSN_Blackhawks/status/2043082415850098831?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 11, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Combine 15 individual big leaps together, and that could add up to one big collective leap for the Hawks. That logic tracks to Ryan Donato, one of few current Hawks old enough to offer an informed perspective.</p><p>"If you're a sponge like I was, or like the young guys we have in here [are], you can learn a lot through a full season," Donato said. "You learn how to maintain your body. You learn how to be a true pro.</p><p>"We're hoping that they take the experiences they had this year, skills that they need to work on, different things that each guy individually wants to do throughout the summer and really try to make the next step. If we do that collectively, it'll be a sign of good things to come."</p><p>When Donato turned pro in 2018 after three years of college, he ran headfirst into the same tsunami of new information and revelations that his Hawks teammates are running into now.</p><p>"It's like, 'There's a level to it, and you have to get to that level,'" he added.</p><p>Imagine <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/30/blackhawks-connor-bedard-nhl-creating-space-nikita-kucherov-jeff-blashill" target="_blank" >Connor Bedard</a> and Frank Nazar level up to the next level of stardom (and continue improving defensively), Anton Frondell gets faster and more comfortable at center and Oliver Moore and Ryan Greene improve their shooting.</p><p>Imagine Nick Lardis learns how to win puck battles, Sam Rinzel and Kevin Korchinski bulk up strength-wise and Alex Crevier and Louis Crevier add more physicality and a little meanness. </p><p>Imagine all of them get more consistent, durable, conditioned and confident, too.</p><p>All of those improvements are attainable, and altogether they would eliminate many weaknesses in this team. That's the vision.</p><p>The worst-case scenario is discovering these kids all are who they are already. Stagnation would be disastrous. It will simply take time to see which reality unfolds.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Older, sicker Americans waiting longer to get into crowded hospitals
entries.summary: <p>An endocrinologist is a doctor specializing in hormone-related diseases, such as, in my case, diabetes. When first diagnosed in late September 2024, I got a crash course in the huge demand for that profession's services. The first endocrinologist I approached wouldn't see me for a year. The second wouldn't see me at all; he was refusing all new patients.</p><p>Figuring I would have to engage another gear if I didn't want to sit around, doing nothing, waiting to go blind, I grabbed my notebook and decided, if I couldn't meet with an endocrinologist as a patient, I'd find out what I needed to know by writing a column. Diabetes affects 40 million Americans; it isn't as if it's a personal affliction.</p><p>That third endocrinologist not only spoke with me immediately — barred doors fly open for publicity — but put me in touch with a colleague who, either through a sea change in my luck or, I suspect, some kind of secret doctor-to-doctor dog whistle, took me under her wing as a patient.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a20000" name="module-a20000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Opinion bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Opinion</h2></div> </div><p>If this strikes you are morally squishy — the journalist pushing to the head of the line — I worried about that, too. But I didn't misrepresent anything; <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2024/10/22/diabetes-type-1-2-diet-health-care-preventive-medicine-obesity" target="_blank" >the column</a> ran in the paper.</p><p>Besides, with health care, you have to be a strong advocate for yourself. Faced with the prospect of letting my condition go unchecked while I hunted with increasingly numb fingers for an endocrinologist with an open slot, I did what I could. At that point, if meeting <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2016/9/22/18364946/politician-morgan-finley-a-monument-to-corruption-has-died" target="_blank" >Morgan Finley</a> in a Cicero motel room and handing over an envelope of cash would have gotten me an appointment, well, I certainly would have considered it.</p><p>I thickly assumed this was a problem inherent to endocrinology. Getting diabetes is easy — I just woke up one morning with Type 1. Medical school is hard. Of course, there's a shortage. Now it turns out I was encountering, not a diabetes-specific bottleneck, but a generalized, widespread condition.</p><p>This week, The Economist published a story with the musical title, <a class="Link" href="https://www.economist.com/international/2026/04/09/hospitals-are-stuck-in-a-deadly-doom-loop" target="_blank" >“Hospitals are stuck in a deadly doom loop.”</a> Turns out the 2020 COVID crisis not only killed millions worldwide and shut down society, but it also "did lasting damage to health-care systems."</p><p>Where? Everywhere, all over the world. What's been damaged? In a nutshell, everything.</p><p>"From admission to discharge, hospital care is now harder to access, takes longer and is of worse quality," the magazine reports. "The resulting toll includes avoidable deaths. Almost everyone is affected: across 18 rich democracies, satisfaction with health-care quality fell sharply after the pandemic and remains well below the pre-pandemic norm."</p><p>Getting an appointment takes forever. As does getting admitted after showing up in the emergency room. Last year, one in 10 patients visiting an emergency room in England had to wait 12 hours or longer before being shown a room.</p><p>And in Chicago, an NBC News Channel 5 report found that Chicago has longer wait times to see a doctor than most American cities — a month to see a primary care doctor. For specialty care, like neurologists, up to five months.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.hospitalstats.org/ER-Wait-Time/Chicago-IL-Metro.htm" target="_blank" >Hospitalstats.org </a>cited wait times at Northwestern Memorial Hospital, between showing up at the ER and being admitted, as approaching six hours; members of the Service Employees International Union held a protest of long wait times and short-staffing outside the Northwestern ER in January. Rush and University of Illinois hospitals both have wait times of over five hours (while the fastest, St. Joseph, Loretto and Thorek, have under three hours).</p><p>Before the pandemic, about 64% of American hospital beds were occupied. Now it's 75%, and in places as high as 88%, there's no room at the inn.</p><p>Why? It's a mystery. The Economist calls it a "productivity puzzle." Hospitals in general have more resources — more money, more staff. But are doing worse.</p><p>One factor is older, sicker patients who delayed getting treatment, because they can't get into hospitals, so are even older and sicker when they do.</p><p>Another suspect is burnout.</p><p>"Pandemic-era stresses increased churn as doctors and nurses resigned, or retired early," The Economist wrote. "Health-care workers today are less experienced and perhaps less productive."</p><p>I've seen my endocrinologist twice over a year and a half. After the first appointment, she gave that secret dog whistle, and I got a second appointment in six months. In the meantime, I saw a nurse practitioner. After the second appointment, the endocrinologist said, "I'd like to see you in three or four months." Knowing better, I told her she'd better alert her front desk, or there wouldn't be a vacancy.</p><p>When I went to schedule my return, the receptionist slotted me in to the next available opening, 364 days in the future.<br></p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: WNBA free-agency update: Sky trade Ariel Atkins for Rickea Jackson
entries.summary: <p>The final domino in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky’s</a> plan to overhaul their roster has fallen. After coring Ariel Atkins, they traded her to the Sparks for forward Rickea Jackson.</p><p>“It’s thrilling to acquire one of the great young talents in this league in Jackson,” general manager Jeff Pagliocca said in a statement. “Rickea is only going to continue to ascend, and the organization is excited to witness that growth firsthand.”</p><p>Jackson averaged 14.7 points per game for the Sparks last season. The 25-year old brings scoring punch and is another young building block to pair with center Kamilla Cardoso.</p><p>A starting lineup could now include Jackson, Cardoso, Skylar Diggins, and Azurá Stevens. The Sky also traded for point guard Jacy Sheldon and resigned Elizabeth Williams and Rachel Banham. That's enough talent to make a playoff run.</p><p>If the Sky are successful this season and Jackson keeps getting better, this trade could help vindicate Pagliocca's riskiest move to date. To get Atkins, he traded away the No. 3 pick in the 2025 draft, which became Sonia Citron, as well as the right to swap picks in 2027.</p><p>That deal looked hairy last season. Citron became an All-Star in her rookie year, while Atkins never fully adapted to her new role as primary scorer. The Mystics also finished ahead of the Sky in the standings, putting the 2027 swap in jeopardy. </p><p>Now the sum total of Pagliocca's moves this offseason make the Sky look more formidable than the Mystics, at least in the short-term. Plus, a Jackson-Cardoso frontcourt might be a better combination than Cardoso and Angel Reese.</p><p>That's the upshot. The downside is losing Atkins. She's an elite defender, a veteran leader, and a two-time All-Star, all things the Sky will miss. Entering her third WNBA season, Jackson still has a lot to prove. </p><p>So do the Sky. </p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Possibilities and obstacles for those seeking an education in Illinois prisons
entries.summary: <div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-1e0000" name="module-1e0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><i>Prisoncast!</i> from WBEZ Chicago 91.5 FM airs <b>Sunday, April 12, from 2 p.m. to 4 p.m.</b> The statewide special broadcast uses the power of radio to connect people inside prisons and jails with their loved ones on the outside.<br><br>The program explores Illinois’ prison system through questions, requests and ideas from people currently and formerly incarcerated in Illinois. It also includes song requests and voicemails from loved ones. Submit yours at <a class="Link" href="mailto:prisoncast@wbez.org" target="_blank" ><u>prisoncast@wbez.org</u></a> or call <b>312-893-2931</b>.</div> </div><p>In a recent visit to Sheridan Correctional Center, 90 minutes southwest of Chicago, Mayor Brandon Johnson spoke with incarcerated students in an hour-long conversation covering a wide range of topics from public education to affordable housing and even peewee football in the suburbs.</p><p>“Take that same energy and help me build an economy that works for everyone so we don’t need prisons,” Johnson said to the students and families of the Northwestern Prison Education Program. Some were dressed in their “visiting gear” — meticulously cleaned, bright white sneakers and crisp prison blues that some had pressed under the mattress in their cells.</p><p>Darvin Henderson, who’s working toward his degree, said he wasn’t raised in an environment where meeting influential politicians felt possible.</p><p>“Those opportunities never even was available,” he said, “and look: I’m in the prison, people would think I’m at the bottom, and yet I’m rubbing elbows with people at the top.”</p><p>The mayor’s visit was organized by Northwestern University. Henderson is one of less than 2% of Illinois’ more than 30,000 incarcerated people enrolled in college-level programs. Years of research show education leads to better outcomes for people getting out of prison. But with only 12 programs in 10 of the 30 Illinois Department of Corrections facilities, getting into a prison college program is impossible for most.</p><p>Education behind bars — its possibilities and obstacles — is the theme of an upcoming special broadcast of<i> Prisoncast!</i> from WBEZ Chicago, a radio program exploring the prison system through questions and requests from people incarcerated in Illinois and loved ones. In the two-hour program on <b>Sunday, April 12, from 2 pm. to 4 p.m.</b>, listeners will hear from inmates, advocates and loved ones on getting an education behind bars and the barriers to continuing college courses once back in the community.</p><p>Also in the broadcast:</p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-f6bac690-351f-11f1-a45e-8b6a340a1ec0"><li>Female inmates finding creative ways to preserve their dignity through beauty routines </li><li>Why Illinois prisons are still providing abysmal health care, despite years of court oversight</li><li>Thoughts on the power of song from inmates enrolled in the Northwestern Prison Education Program</li><li>One incarcerated man’s life-changing experience with prison education — and why he says Illinois needs more of it</li></ul><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sun-times-and-wbez-staff" >Chicago Sun-Times and WBEZ staff </a></strong> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div><p></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-780000" name="module-780000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Why we make <i>Prisoncast!</i></h3><br>The United States has the <a class="Link" href="https://www.prisonpolicy.org/profiles/US.html" target="_blank" ><u>highest incarceration rate</u></a> in the world. Nearly half of all Americans have had an immediate family member in prison or jail. Yet prison systems, including Illinois’, are designed to be opaque.<br><br>More than 40% of people locked up there were convicted in Cook County, and the vast majority will one day be released. <i>Prisoncast!</i> utilizes a uniquely uncensorable medium — the statewide public radio network — to get practical news and information to people incarcerated here, and to share their untold stories with the outside world.<br><br>Everything on the show comes directly from audience requests, which provides a point of connection for those behind bars and people on the outside. It also provides transparency into a taxpayer-funded system through reporting and investigations on prison conditions, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health/2026/03/02/health-care-illinois-prisons-consent-decree" target="_blank" ><u>healthcare</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/public-safety/2026/02/06/no-good-time-for-you-the-recordkeeping-snafu-keeping-some-illinoisans-in-prison-longer-than-they-should-be" target="_blank" ><u>state policies</u></a>.<br><br>We know many people in prison have caused profound hurt. <i>Prisoncast!</i> doesn’t shy away from that. Neither do many of the people we’ve interviewed.<br><br>Our approach in producing the show is to understand the people and systems at play. Through this exploration, the public — and incarcerated listeners — may find solutions and inspiration to contribute to a safer community for all.<br><br><h4>How can I listen? </h4><br>In the Chicago area, you can hear the next show live on <b>WBEZ 91.5 FM on Sunday, April 12, at 2 p.m</b>. The show also airs on every <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/2024/12/16/aboutprisoncast" target="_blank" ><u>Illinois Public Radio station</u></a> in the state. If you have a loved one who’s incarcerated, they can listen on an old-school radio or on their tablet’s FM radio app.<br><br><h4>What if I missed the radio broadcast? </h4><br>All previous <i>Prisoncast! </i>episodes, newsletters, investigations and stories are at <a class="Link" href="http://wbez.org/prisoncast" target="_blank" ><u>wbez.org/prisoncast</u></a>. People in Illinois prisons and jails can access our content for free on the Edovo app on their ICS tablets, though it can take a couple weeks for content to upload there. We’re also launching the new <i>Prisoncast!</i> podcast on April 20.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Chicago Sun-Times and WBEZ staff

entries.title: WNBA free agency update: Sky get tougher defensively, sign DiJonai Carrington
entries.summary: <p>One missing piece from the Sky's roster overhaul? A stopper on the wing. </p><p>They solved that Sunday by signing DiJonai Carrington, a former All-Defense selection.</p><p>“We’re thrilled to bring DiJonai to the Sky,” general manager Jeff Pagliocca said in a statement. “She brings speed, defense, versatility and a fierce competitive spirit, and we can’t wait to see her electrify Skytown.”</p><p>She signed a one-year deal with the Sky, according to ESPN. </p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Tanner Murray’s first career home run helps White Sox salvage series split with Royals
entries.summary: <p>KANSAS CITY, Mo. — The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> finally found some offense Sunday with their first home runs in a week to overcome a tough afternoon for the pitching staff, beating the Royals 6-5 to salvage a four-game series split in Kansas City.</p><p>Following a three-hour rain delay, <b>Tanner Murray</b>’s first career home run in the second inning ended a 21-inning scoring drought for the Sox. The rookie’s two-run blast plated <b>Colson Montgomery</b>, who had doubled off Royals starter <b>Noah Cameron</b>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Tanner Murray making his first career home run count! <a href="https://t.co/3TVDPXKkIA">pic.twitter.com/3TVDPXKkIA</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2043441921398338047?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 12, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>After<b> Grant Taylor</b>’s flawless two-inning <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/06/grant-taylor-opener-white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>opening appearance</u></a>, <b>Sean Newcomb</b> followed in the third inning and soon gave way to <b>Jonathan Cannon</b>, who looked off from the start.</p><p>Cannon’s first pitch since being called up from Charlotte was wild, allowing the two baserunners he inherited to advance, before walking one batter to load the bases and two more to tie the game up. He exited the game with hip irritation.</p><p>After two straight shutout losses for the Sox, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/10/anthony-kay-jac-caglianone-white-sox-royals-hit-by-pitch?55" target="_blank" ><u>division rivals</u></a> swapped leads with Montgomery blasting a two-run bomb in the fourth, and both bullpens struggling to find the strike zone.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Stay fair. It will! <a href="https://t.co/4k0k11jsQ7">pic.twitter.com/4k0k11jsQ7</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2043452090341527759?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 12, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Sox outfielder <b>Dustin Harris</b> opened the seventh with a pinch-hit double off the right-field wall and eventually scored to put the Sox ahead on a bases-loaded wild pitch from KC reliever <b>John Schreiber</b>.</p><p>Harris later went airborne to at least extra bases from Royals second baseman <b>Michael Massey</b>, a Palos Park resident, whose eighth-inning drive fell just a few feet short of a game-tying homer.</p><p>Montgomery was in visible pain after taking a 95 mph sinker off the forearm in the eighth, but he stayed in the game.</p><h3><b>Mr. Smith goes to Charlotte</b></h3><p><b>Shane Smith</b> had an uneven outing Sunday in his first start at Triple-A Charlotte after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports/2026/04/08/white-sox-option-opening-day-starter-shane-smith-to-triple-a-charlotte" target="_blank" ><u>being demoted</u></a>.</p><p>The Sox’ Opening Day starter and 2025 All-Star gave up three first-inning runs on three hits, a walk and a hit batter, but settled in for three innings after that with five strikeouts.</p><p>The Sox shipped Smith east to straighten things out after he gave up 10 earned runs over three short starts. The rest of the rotation has mostly <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/11/davis-martin-white-sox-strong-start" target="_blank" ><u>looked solid</u></a> since a brutal opening week.</p><p>In his first game of a rehabilitation stint with the Knights, outfielder <b>Everson Pereira</b> smacked a 405-foot home run.</p><h3><b>Rebuild remnants</b></h3><p>One-time Sox rebuilding centerpiece <b>Eloy Jimenez</b> was called up Sunday by the Blue Jays for his first big-league action since 2024. He went 2-4 with two singles and two strikeouts as Toronto’s DH.</p><p><b>Luis Robert Jr.</b> has hit.319/.458/.447 with two homers, seven RBI and 12 walks over his first 14 games with the Mets. <b>Luisangel Acuna</b>, the Sox’ main return for Robert, entered play Sunday slashing .214/.244/.214 with four stolen bases.</p><p><b>Yoan Moncada</b> (.125/.340/.250 with a homer and four RBI) hasn’t fared as well for the Angels.</p><p>The Sox face the Angels at home April 27-29 and in Anaheim May 4-6. They visit Toronto July 17-19 and welcome the Mets to Rate Field Aug. 21-23.</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Jameson Taillon fights the wind, gives Cubs six innings
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> starter <b>Jameson Taillon </b>saw what everybody else did Sunday at Wrigley Field: the flags snapping straight out.</p><p>“It’s not ideal for what I do and my profile,” <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/03/31/cubs-right-hander-jameson-taillon-delivers-decent-start-in-cubs-2-0-loss-to-angels" >Taillon</a> said. “A lot of fly balls, but it’s part of playing here. I’ve [benefited] plenty of days when the wind blows in.</p><p>Taillon gave up three home runs, including <b>Oneil Cruz’s </b>drive to begin the game. The most damaging one was the second-inning <b>Brandon Lowe </b>grand slam.</p><p>And what bugged Taillon about the grand slam was putting runners on base and falling behind hitters to get into a bad spot, not the pitch itself.</p><p>“The homer was kind of, whatever,” Taillon said. “I understand on a day like today it can happen, but getting us in that situation with the bases loaded was not ideal.”</p><p>Lowe, who added a fifth-inning home run, hit the grand slam with a 97.3 mph exit velocity and 39-degree launch angle. On many days, especially early in the season, that’s a fly ball to right. Sunday was unseasonably warm and the wind was not a pitcher’s friend.</p><p>Somewhat counterintuitively, Taillon said it’s more important to attack the zone in Sunday’s conditions.</p><p>“The lesson to be learned is, no matter what the wind is doing it’s on me to be aggressive and still fill up the zone and not let guys get on and get in bad counts,” Taillon said.</p><p>After giving up five runs over the first two innings, Taillon bore down and gave up one over his last four. Taillon’s line won’t help his final statistics, but he gave the Cubs the length they needed.</p><p>“After [the second] inning, it can’t really get any worse,” Taillon said. “There’s almost a little bit of weird freedom in that. ‘I’ve got nothing to lose, let’s be super-aggressive here and see what we can make out of this day.’”</p><h2><b>Harvey hurt</b></h2><p>When the Cubs signed reliever <b>Hunter Harvey </b>to one-year, $6 million contract, they knew his injury history. In 2025 with the Royals, Harvey only pitched 12 times and was twice placed on the injured list, adding to his unfortunately lengthy resume of health problems.</p><p>It didn’t take long for another issue to crop up this year, adding another challenge to the bullpen after <b>Phil Maton </b>was placed on the IL on April 10.</p><p>Harvey was placed on the 15-day IL with right triceps inflammation, retroactive to April 9. The 31-year-old righthander pitched April 3 at Cleveland, was rested until April 8 at Tampa Bay and wasn’t recovering, leading to Sunday’s move.</p><p>“We’re trying to get stuff before it gets too bad here,” manager <b>Craig Counsell</b> said. “Hopefully, it’s just a little bit of soreness in the triceps and we can get rid of it in 2-3 weeks and move on.”</p><p>Counsell said Harvey’s past was a factor in the Cubs being conservative with his health. The way Harvey was recovering, Counsell added, sent up red flags that the Cubs needed to be careful.</p><p>For now, Harvey is not scheduled to undergo any imaging on his arm.</p><p>To replace Harvey, the Cubs selected the contract of lefthander <b>Charlie Barnes </b>from Triple-A Iowa. Barnes was 3-0 with a 2.38 ERA in three games with Iowa.</p>
entries.author: Brian Sandalow

entries.title: Slumping Michael Busch helps Cubs rally past Pirates for 7-6 win
entries.summary: <p>Michael Busch and the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> really needed Sunday.</p><p>Benched after an 0 for 30 slump, Busch’s eighth-inning pinch-hit single led to the Cubs tying the game. Carson Kelly’s ninth-inning single gave the Cubs a 7-6 win over the Pirates, helping them avoid a sweep after two games of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/04/11/cubs-reliever-caleb-thielbar-has-key-error-but-why-was-matt-shaw-playing-first-base" >missed opportunities and poor situational hitting</a>.</p><p>“That win was pretty big,” Busch said.</p><p>For the first time in a while, Busch played a part in a Cubs victory.</p><p>Expected to be a key cog in the Cubs offense after a strong 2025 when he hit 34 home runs with a .343 on-base percentage, Busch’s start to 2026 hasn’t been ideal.</p><p>Entering Sunday’s game, Busch was hitting .118 with an OPS-plus of 15. His advanced stats didn’t indicate he’s having bad luck – Busch’s average exit velocity of 87.0 mph was in the 26th percentile of MLB and his 5.0% barrel rate was in the 33rd percentile.</p><p>“It’s been pretty frustrating, but putting one foot in front of the other each day and continuing to work,” Busch said before the game. “Starting to feel a little better as of late, but obviously not getting the results I want.”</p><p>Busch’s hit Sunday wasn’t a screamer – it left his bat at just 68.2 mph – but he and the Cubs weren’t too concerned about that. They were glad to see him break through.</p><p>“It lets you take a big exhale,” manager Craig Counsell said. “It doesn’t give you anything tomorrow or the next day but it lets you take a big exhale and you contribute to a win. For every guy in there, you desperately want to do that.”</p><p>Busch was not the only Cubs player who helped them recover from an early 5-0 deficit.</p><p>Dansby Swanson homered in the third and made two alert plays on the bases. With the bases loaded and one out in the seventh, Alex Bregman popped to the Pirates’ Brandon Lowe at second. Lowe made the catch but fell backward, allowing Swanson to score. In the eighth, Swanson came around from first to score the tying run on Busch’s single when Pittsburgh left fielder Bryan Reynolds made an errant throw back into the infield.</p><p>Swanson said he’s been taught his whole life to run with his eyes up and on the ball. That gave the Cubs two important runs Sunday.</p><p>“Your instincts can tell you to go quicker than someone can tell you to go, for you to listen and then be able to go,” Swanson said. “A lot of it is just enjoyment. I get a lot of joy out of running bases.”</p><p>There hadn’t been a lot of joy around the Cubs during the first two games with Pittsburgh. They were shut out Friday and frustrated Saturday, going 1 for 15 with runners in scoring position and stranding 16 runners on base in a 4-3, 11-inning loss.</p><p>From the outside, Sunday looked like it would be no different. The Cubs were down and not maximizing opportunities.</p><p>Inside the dugout, the vibe was different. Relievers Riley Martin and Ethan Roberts combined for two scoreless innings, buying the Cubs time until they rallied.</p><p>“I never saw our guys waver or falter today,” said starter Jameson Taillon, who pitched six innings and allowed six runs, including Lowe’s second-inning grand slam.</p><p>Because of Busch and others, the Cubs ended a rough series in a good mood.</p><p>“Every day is a must-win,” Kelly said. “We just continue to keep putting good days on top of good days. We control what we can control and just continue to keep hitting the ball hard, play good defense and pitch well.”</p>
entries.author: Brian Sandalow

entries.title: Out with the old, in with the new: Sky trade Ariel Atkins for Rickea Jackson — and begin again
entries.summary: <p>Out with the old, in with the new. </p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> said goodbye to shooting guard Ariel Atkins, trading her to the Sparks for up-and-coming forward Rickea Jackson.</p><p>It could be for the best.</p><p>Atkins is an excellent player — an elite defender, a veteran leader, a two-time All-Star. But Jackson, 25, has a ton of upside and already brings more of a scoring punch.</p><p>She averaged 14.7 points per game for the Sparks last season, only her second year in the league. Pair that with the Sky's top three free-agency additions — Skylar Diggins, Azura Stevens and DiJonai Carrington — and you're looking at a playoff-caliber lineup.</p><p>At least on paper, it might be the best-looking roster since 2023.</p><p>Remember 2023 — the last time the Sky made the playoffs? The core group from the 2021 championship had split up, but instead of rebuilding, then-general manager James Wade put together a legitimately competitive roster.</p><p>Remember that starting lineup — Courtney Williams, Marina Mabrey, Kahleah Copper, Alanna Smith and Elizabeth Williams? There could have — there should have — been a foundation to build around.</p><p>But within a year and a half, everyone besides Elizabeth Williams was gone. Mabrey and Copper requested trades. Smith and Courtney Williams found greener pastures elsewhere.</p><p>So they began again.</p><p>In 2024, the Sky drafted Kamilla Cardoso No. 3 overall and traded up to get Angel Reese at No. 7, touting them as the future of the franchise. (And don't forget the bet on Chennedy Carter.) In 2025, they traded substantial draft capital to acquire Atkins, whom they also envisioned as part of a longer-term core.</p><p>Now Cardoso is the only one from that group who remains.</p><p>So they begin again.</p><p>They've had some good beginnings.</p><p>But they haven't been able to follow through. It's one thing to put together a talented roster. It's another to keep a core together — the way the Liberty have, the way the Aces have — and contend for a championship.</p><p>Will this time be different? </p><p>If it is, the next couple of years could look something like this: <br></p><ul id="rte-2ae30640-36c3-11f1-a913-0b19c90e6597"><li>The Sky make the playoffs in 2026. </li><li>Jackson and Cardoso develop into All-Stars by 2027. </li><li>Jackson and Cardoso extend their contracts, solidifying a long-term core.</li><li>The front office makes smart moves around them in the intervening seasons, and suddenly, the Sky are contending for another championship.</li></ul><p>If it's just the same old cycle, then the next couple of years will look like this: <br></p><ul id="rte-2ae30641-36c3-11f1-a913-0b19c90e6597"><li>The Sky make the playoffs in 2026, or they don't — it doesn't really matter either way. Top players start peeling off. Some old pieces get flipped for pretty good new pieces, but ultimately the Sky find themselves right back where they started.</li><li>They begin again.</li></ul>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Evanston pastor resigns amid accusations he used ‘substantial’ parish funds for personal finances
entries.summary: <p>Cardinal Blase J. Cupich announced the resignation of Rev. Kenneth Anderson, pastor of Evanston’s St. John Henry Newman Parish, after church officials found Anderson used parish funds for personal expenses.</p><p>In a letter Friday to the parish community — which includes St. Athanasius Church and St. Joan of Arc Church — Cupich said an archdiocese review determined Anderson had “violated a number of core Archdiocesan policies” regarding his stewardship of the parish’s finances.</p><p>The cardinal said Anderson had diverted “substantial” parish funds into a separate bank account that was used to pay personal expenses.</p><p>Anderson agreed to pay back any funds used for his personal expenses “when the full accounting is complete,” Cupich said in the letter.</p><p>“We take seriously our duty to steward the resources you provide and uphold best practices in their management,” Cupich wrote. “Your trust means a great deal to me and I appreciate the patience you have shown as we conducted the review. We will take steps with the parish leadership to remedy any deficiencies or lack of controls.”</p><p>The archdiocese, the arm of the Catholic Church for Cook and Lake counties led by Cupich, declined to comment further or answer specific details about the finances of the parish and how much Anderson was accused of misspending, citing the ongoing investigation.</p><p>Neither the parish nor Anderson immediately responded to requests for comment.</p><p>The church’s placement board is expected to nominate a permanent successor by July 1, Cupich said in his letter.</p><p>Anderson was appointed to lead the parish in July 2022, when the church merged St. Athanasius and St. Joan of Arc as part of the <a class="Link" href="https://www.archchicago.org/news-release/-/article/2022/01/29/archdiocese-of-chicago-provides-update-on-the-evanston-renew-my-church-grouping" target="_blank" ><u>Renew My Church program</u></a>.</p><p>He had initially been allowed to stay on as pastor when the investigation was first announced in another letter to the community from Cupich last month.</p><p> Parish bulletins show it took in more than $54,000 in donations in February, about $4,400 more than needed to cover its budget; nearly $20,000 was donated on one Sunday alone.</p><p>Last July, Anderson wrote in the church’s bulletin that it was $41,000 short on offertory giving in the first half of the year. As part of an effort to make up the deficit, he and others at the church started a “Balance Our Budget” campaign, which had raised nearly $77,000 by the end of last July.</p><p>“As can be seen, the Church is still substantially behind in overall income vs expenses,” Anderson wrote in the <a class="Link" href="https://container.parishesonline.com/bulletins/01/0534/20250720B.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>July 20, 2025, bulletin</u></a>. “The staff has been very dedicated to maintaining any unnecessary expenditures and keeping a very tight income vs. expense. Simply, our overall income from all sources is not enough to cover expenses. This has been the case for the past 3 years. Please consider a contribution to B.O.B. if you can.”</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p><a class="Link" href="https://container.parishesonline.com/bulletins/01/0534/20240630B.pdf" target="_blank" ><u></u></a></p><p>The Rev. Gerald Gunderson will come out of retirement to serve as an interim administrator, and officials asked Bishop Timothy O’Malley to meet with church officials to best determine a way to “rebuild the trust of parishioners,” Cupich said in the letter.</p><p>The archdiocese announced the investigation March 30, and the Rev. Wayne Watts, pastor of Saints Joseph and Francis Xavier Parish in nearby Wilmette, had been managing the parish’s finances in the interim, according to prior letters from Cupich.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/22/st-hubert-families-push-to-keep-suburban-catholic-school-open-amid-six-school-closures" >Archdiocese has ‘no plan’ to reopen 6 Catholic schools despite families’ push</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Violet Miller
